elements.sent-2411-001: Vision-Language (V-L) pre-trained models such as CLIP show prominent capabilities in various downstream tasks.
elements.sent-2411-002: Despite this promise, V-L models are notoriously limited by their inherent social biases.
elements.sent-2411-003: A typical demonstration is that V-L models often produce biased predictions against specific groups of people, significantly undermining their real-world applicability.
elements.sent-2411-004: Existing approaches endeavor to mitigate the social bias problem in V-L models by removing biased attribute information from model embeddings.
elements.sent-2411-005: However, after our revisiting of these methods, we find that their bias removal is frequently accompanied by greatly compromised V-L alignment capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-006: We then reveal that this performance degradation stems from the unbalanced debiasing in image and text embeddings.
elements.sent-2411-007: To address this issue, we propose a novel V-L debiasing framework to align image and text biases followed by removing them from both modalities.
elements.sent-2411-008: By doing so, our method achieves multi-modal bias mitigation while maintaining the V-L alignment in the debiased embeddings.
elements.sent-2411-009: Additionally, we advocate a new evaluation protocol that can 1) holistically quantify the model debiasing and V-L alignment ability, and 2) evaluate the generalization of social bias removal models.
elements.sent-2411-010: We believe this work will offer new insights and guidance for future studies addressing the social bias problem in CLIP.
elements.sent-2411-011: Our code can be found at \href{https://github.com/haoyusimon/VL_Debiasing {link .}}
elements.sent-2411-012: Recently, Vision-Language (V-L) pre-trained models such as CLIP~\cite{Radford-2021-clip and BLIP~\cite{junnan-2022-blip have gained dominant popularity.}}
elements.sent-2411-013: The pre-training using large-scale image-text pairs from the web aids the alignment between visual and linguistic semantics.
elements.sent-2411-014: The alignment has led to remarkable zero-shot performance across a diverse range of applications such as image classification~\cite{deng-2009-imagenet and cross-modal retrieval~\cite{plummer-2015-flickr .}}
elements.sent-2411-015: Despite this capability, a pressing issue of social bias significantly impedes V-L models' real-world deployment.
elements.sent-2411-016: In particular, these V-L models frequently develop associations of neutral concepts with people's sensitive attributes, leading to biased model outputs against specific social groups~\cite{wang-etal-2021-gender .}
elements.sent-2411-017: This problem, as hidden in their learned embeddings, primarily originates from the stereotypes~\cite{birhane-2021-multimodal and spurious correlations~\cite{singla-2022-core in the training data.}}
elements.sent-2411-018: Existing social debiasing\footnote{In this paper, we use the terms social debiasing and social bias removal interchangeably.}
elements.sent-2411-019: They involve either learning a fair module~\cite{berg-etal-2022-prompt,seth-2023-dear, hirota-2024-saner, kong-2024-ttbias or directly manipulating the embedding vectors (through projection~\cite{chuang-2023-debiasing, dehdashtian-2024-fairerclip, gerych-2024-bendvlm or feature clipping based on mutual information~\cite{wang-etal-2021-gender ).}}}
elements.sent-2411-020: However, after revisiting several representative approaches, we uncover one significant drawback: these debiasing approaches greatly compromise the V-L alignment ability in their produced embeddings - a phenomenon we term over-debiasing.

elements.sent-2411-022: One can see that despite the alleviation of social bias level\footnote{A smaller MaxSkew indicates less social bias in models.}

elements.sent-2411-023: We then delve into the underlying rationale of the over-debiasing problem.
elements.sent-2411-024: Our first observation is that social biases exist in both image and text embeddings of the CLIP model (refer to Fig.~\ref{fig:vis for more details).}
elements.sent-2411-025: Nevertheless, most previous approaches debias one modality only, leaving the other one untouched~\cite{berg-etal-2022-prompt,chuang-2023-debiasing,seth-2023-dear .}
elements.sent-2411-026: CLIP-clip~\cite{wang-etal-2021-gender attempts to debias both modalities by removing the same biased dimensions of image and text embedding vectors.}
elements.sent-2411-027: However, as indicated in our second observation (Sect.~\ref{sec:3.2 ), removing the exact same dimensions of text embeddings as those of vision in CLIP-clip fails to reliably debias as the image and text biases are often misaligned.}
elements.sent-2411-028: Motivated by the above observations, in this paper, we propose a novel V-L debiasing method to jointly debias both image and text modalities.
elements.sent-2411-029: Our proposed method involves a bias alignment and a counterfactual debiasing operation.
elements.sent-2411-030: Specifically, the bias alignment operation aligns the bias embeddings between image and text before debiasing.
elements.sent-2411-031: This distinguishes our method from CLIP-clip which applies the same debiasing to both modalities without alignment.
elements.sent-2411-032: Our second contribution involves triplets consisting of two oppositely biased text prompts with the same neutral information and one biased image.
elements.sent-2411-033: Under this context, we design a counterfactual debiasing objective that pulls debiased text prompts closer to remove biases while preserving the neutral information.
elements.sent-2411-034: Our method is also enabled to maintain the V-L alignment in debiased embeddings by combining debiasing with the original contrastive learning objectives.
elements.sent-2411-035: Additionally, we develop two new evaluation protocols for social bias removal in CLIP.
elements.sent-2411-036: We first design a new metric called Alignment and Bias Level Evaluation (ABLE), holistically evaluating the effectiveness of model debiasing and downstream V-L alignment.
elements.sent-2411-037: Thereafter, we propose to leverage out-of-domain datasets to test the generalization ability of social debiasing models, in contrast to most previous approaches using in-domain evaluation only.
elements.sent-2411-038: When comparing with several baselines, we observe that our proposed method demonstrates significant advantages across various backbones including ViT-B/16, ViT-B/32, ViT-L/14, and ViT-H/14.
elements.sent-2411-039: Furthermore, our method allows for joint debiasing of multiple types of social biases, making it more applicable to real-world debiasing requirements.
elements.sent-2411-040: In summary, this paper makes the following three contributions:.
elements.sent-2411-041: We revisit existing social debiasing methods in CLIP and underline their over-debiasing problem.
elements.sent-2411-042: The innate reason for this problem is further illuminated from the perspective of misaligned V-L debiasing.
elements.sent-2411-043: We propose a novel joint V-L debiasing method to address the over-debiasing problem.
elements.sent-2411-044: Our method effectively mitigates both image and text biases despite their different distributions while maintaining V-L alignment in the debiased embeddings.
elements.sent-2411-045: We advocate two new evaluation protocols to comprehensively evaluate the bias reduction and V-L alignment in a social debiasing method.
elements.sent-2411-047: The social bias problem (prediction inclining to specific groups of gender, age, and race) has been long a pressing concern in various realms including vision-centric, language-centric, and V-L models.
elements.sent-2411-048: In the vision domain, social biases are measured in terms of fairness metrics such as Demographic Parity~\cite{feldman-2015-dp and Equalized Odds~\cite{hardt-2016-eo .}}
elements.sent-2411-049: These metrics evaluate whether the model performance in downstream tasks, e.g., classification, varies significantly when applied to different social groups.
elements.sent-2411-050: Pertaining to language models, early work probes social biases in word-level embeddings using the World Embedding Association Test (WEAT)~\cite{caliskan-2017-weat and projection on bias direction~\cite{bolu-2016-word_projection .}}
elements.sent-2411-051: Later, the development of pre-trained language models~\cite{devlin-etal-2019-bert,radford-2019-gpt motivates the study on the sentence-level social bias, leading to benchmarks such as Sentence Encoder Association Test (SEAT)~\cite{may-etal-2019-seat , SteroSet~\cite{nadeem-etal-2021-stereoset and CrowS-Pairs~\cite{nangia-etal-2020-crows .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-052: Additionally, for V-L models like CLIP, fairness is commonly evaluated based on the proportion of retrieved images within different groups~\cite{geyik-2019-linkedin,wang-etal-2021-gender or the diversity of the images produced by generative models conditioned on CLIP text embeddings~\cite{chuang-2023-debiasing .}}
elements.sent-2411-053: Some work~\cite{Dehdashtian_2024_CVPR also characterizes accuracy-fairness trade-offs of CLIP.. Social Bias Removal.}
elements.sent-2411-054: To alleviate social bias problems in vision models, existing approaches typically add debiasing objectives~\cite{alvi-2019-confusion_loss,tianlu-2019-adversarial or learn an additional fair module~\cite{mengnan-2021-fairness_module,kim-2019-multiacc .}}
elements.sent-2411-055: Unlike those in the vision-centric domain, social debiasing on language models focuses more on word-level and sentence-level embeddings.
elements.sent-2411-056: For instance, Hard-Debiasing~\cite{bolu-2016-word_projection initially identifies a bias subspace on word embeddings.}
elements.sent-2411-057: The biases are projected onto this subspace and subsequently removed.
elements.sent-2411-058: Similar projection-based methods such as SentenceDebias~\cite{liang-etal-2020-sentDebias and Iterative Nullspace Projection~\cite{ravfogel-etal-2020-inlp along with other methods~\cite{pengyu-2021-fairfil,schick-2021-self-debias,kellie-2021-dropout_debias are developed to remove sentence-level biases.}}}
elements.sent-2411-059: In the V-L domain, existing CLIP social debiasing methods remove the sensitive attribute information from image and text embeddings either through direct vector manipulation or training a fair module.
elements.sent-2411-060: Vector manipulation methods involve applying a projection with closed-form solutions~\cite{chuang-2023-debiasing, dehdashtian-2024-fairerclip, gerych-2024-bendvlm or removing features sharing the largest mutual information with sensitive attribute labels~\cite{wang-etal-2021-gender .}}
elements.sent-2411-061: Differently, other approaches train a fair module with an adversarial loss~\cite{berg-etal-2022-prompt or bias-neutralising objectives~\cite{wang-2022-fairclip, kong-2024-ttbias, hirota-2024-saner , mostly using fairness datasets consisting of annotated images with different attributes such as FairFace \cite{kar-2021-fairface and UTKFace \cite{zhang-2017-utkface .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-062: Most CLIP social debiasing approaches focus on only one modality.
elements.sent-2411-063: It is worth noting that CLIP-clip~\cite{wang-etal-2021-gender attempts multi-modal debiasing.}
elements.sent-2411-064: Specifically, CLIP-clip first locates the dimensions in image embedding vectors which are mostly biased based on the mutual information between these dimensions and bias attributes.
elements.sent-2411-065: It then removes these dimensions in image embeddings and their corresponding dimensions in text embeddings.
elements.sent-2411-066: This operation assumes that dimensions containing more biases in images are also biased in the corresponding text embeddings, which, however, appears less convincing as indicated in our empirical findings.
elements.sent-2411-067: General Bias Removal emphasises on addressing more general biases in models.
elements.sent-2411-068: It involves strategies to minimize spurious correlations between predictions and specific attributes in the training data to achieve group robustness.

elements.sent-2411-123: To mitigate this problem, we propose a novel dual-modal bias alignment-then-removal method.

elements.sent-2411-126: We train this module while keeping the original CLIP model frozen.
elements.sent-2411-127: Specifically, we input the CLIP embeddings of a sampled batch of $N$ biased text-image pairs into the bias alignment module which outputs the aligned text and image bias embeddings: \begin{equation}\phi(t_i) \leftarrow BA(f(t_i); \theta_ba); \psi(v_i) \leftarrow BA(g(v_i); \theta_ba),\end{equation} where $\mathrm{BA (.; \theta_\text{ba )$ denotes our bias alignment module, parameterized by $\theta_\text{ba}$.}}

elements.sent-2411-131: Instead, we propose a novel approach to circumvent this problem.

elements.sent-2411-139: Complementary to $\mathcal{L _{ba}$, we propose an additional counterfactual debiasing objective to supervise dual-modal debiasing.}
elements.sent-2411-140: For each $t_i$, we obtain its counterfactual embedding $t_i'$ by replacing the attribute keyword in the original text prompt.
elements.sent-2411-141: Both $\overline{\phi (t_i)$ and $\overline{\phi (t_i')$ are expected to express the same neutral concept if debiased correctly.}}
elements.sent-2411-142: Our counterfactual debiasing objective is designed to pull $\overline{\phi (t_i)$ and $\overline{\phi (t_i')$ closer in the embedding space while maintaining their V-L alignment capabilities as those of $f(t)$.}}
elements.sent-2411-143: The text counterfactual debiasing loss is expressed as a cross-entropy loss between text-to-image similarities of the debiased embeddings and the original embeddings: \begin{equation} \mathcal{L}_{cd}^t = -\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^N \left[\sum\limits_{v\in\mathcal{V}_q}s_t(t_i, v, \mathcal{V}_q)\log{\overline{s}_t(a(t_i, t_i'), v, \mathcal{V}_q)}\right]{ ,} { \tag{7}}\end{equation} where $a(t_i, t_i') = \beta t_i + (1-\beta)t_i'$ is an alternating function with $\beta\sim\text{Bernoulli (0.5)$, $s_t(.,.,.)$ and $\overline{s _t(.,.,.)$ denote the text-to-image similarity of original text embedding and debiased text embedding, respectively.}}
elements.sent-2411-144: We calculate $s_t(.,.,.)$ following Eqn.~\ref{eq:1 and define $\overline{s _t(.,.,.)$ similarly as follows: \begin{equation} \overline{s}_t(t_j, v_k, \mathcal{V}_q) = \frac{\exp(\langle \overline{\phi}(t_j), g(v_k) \rangle / \tau)}{\sum\limits_{v\in\mathcal{V}_q} \exp(\langle \overline{\phi}(t_j), g(v) \rangle / \tau)}. { \tag{8}}\end{equation} We leverage $\mathcal{L ^t_{cd}$ to i) inherit the original V-L alignment capability, and ii) bridge the embedding gap of two text embeddings with the same concept yet different attributes.}}}
elements.sent-2411-145: In this way, the social bias between different attributes can be accordingly alleviated.
elements.sent-2411-146: For image embeddings, a similar loss is applied to align the image bias removal with that of text and subsequently maintain the V-L alignment of the debiased image embeddings with text embeddings: \begin{equation} \mathcal{L}_{cd}^v = -\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^N \left[\sum\limits_{t\in\mathcal{T}_q}s_v(t, v_i,\mathcal{T}_q)\log{\overline{s}_v(t, v_i,\mathcal{T}_q)}\right]{ ,} { \tag{9}}\end{equation}which does not involve counterfactual images due to their inaccessibility\footnote{Although counterfactual image embeddings can be created using generative models, we abandon such an operation due to computing resource and generation unfaithfulness reasons.. .}
elements.sent-2411-147: Combining both components, the final counterfactual debiasing loss is $\mathcal{L _{cd = (\mathcal{L _{cd ^t + \mathcal{L _{cd ^v)/2$.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-148: For training, our two training objectives are combined as $\mathcal{L = \alpha\mathcal{L _{cd + (1-\alpha)\mathcal{L _{ba}$ with a hyperparameter $\alpha \in [0, 1]$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-149: During inference in downstream tasks, our optimized $\mathrm{BA (.; \theta_\text{ba )$ can be used as a plug-and-play module in CLIP and produce the debiased embeddings $(\overline{\phi (t), \overline{\psi (v))$ of $(t, v)$, respectively expressed as: \begin{equation}\overline{\phi}(t) \leftarrow f(t) - BA(f(t); \theta_ba);{\tag{10}} \overline{\psi}(v) \leftarrow g(v) - BA(g(v); \theta_ba).{\tag{11}}\end{equation}.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-150: For model training, we utilised FairFace~\cite{kar-2021-fairface and UTKFace~\cite{zhang-2017-utkface training subsets containing human face images with gender, age and race labels.}}
elements.sent-2411-151: Unlike previous studies, for a more comprehensive evaluation, we employed both in-domain and out-of-domain fairness evaluations by interchangeably using another test set for the current trained model evaluation.
elements.sent-2411-152: A recent dataset FACET~\cite{gusta-2023-facet was also used to introduce additional out-of-domain photos of full-body images.}
elements.sent-2411-153: Through these datasets, we evaluated both the fairness and generalizability of debiased models.
elements.sent-2411-154: Moreover, to evaluate the V-L task performance of debiased CLIP, we used the ImageNet-1K~\cite{deng-2009-imagenet and Flickr-1K~\cite{plummer-2015-flickr for classification and retrieval tasks, respectively.}}

elements.sent-2411-155: Following existing work~\cite{berg-etal-2022-prompt,seth-2023-dear , we leveraged retrieval-based metrics, mean $\text{MaxSkew @k$ and mean $\text{NDKL @k$, which respectively represent the largest unfair advantage and the average unfair advantage given to images with a certain attribute in the retrieval task.}}}
elements.sent-2411-156: For both metrics, a smaller value implies more fairness of the model.. V-L Alignment Metrics.
elements.sent-2411-157: We report the zero-shot performance on ImageNet-1k classification task~\cite{deng-2009-imagenet (Top-1 and Top-5 accuracies) and the Flickr-1k retrieval tasks~\cite{plummer-2015-flickr (Recall@5 scores for image-to-text retrieval (TR) and text-to-image retrieval (IR)).}}
elements.sent-2411-159: The aforementioned evaluation metrics either evaluate the level of fairness or the V-L alignment of debiasing methods.
elements.sent-2411-160: However, we care about the holistic assessment of social bias removal that covers the above two aspects.
elements.sent-2411-161: Inspired by the F1 Score for imbalanced classification problems, we advocate an Alignment and Bias Level Evaluation (ABLE) metric, defined as, \begin{equation}{ \mathrm{ABLE} = \frac{2}{\frac{1}{acc} + \frac{1}{\exp(-\text{MaxSkew}@k)}}, }{\tag{12}} \end{equation} which is the harmonic mean of the zero-shot ImageNet Top-1 accuracy $acc$ and the negative exponential of $\text{MaxSkew @k$, representing the V-L alignment and fairness level, respectively.}
elements.sent-2411-162: Its range is within [0, 1], and a larger value denotes a better model.
elements.sent-2411-163: Compared Baselines We compared our approach with the original undebiased CLIP and two other publicly available debiasing baselines, CLIP-clip~\cite{wang-etal-2021-gender and Biased-prompts~\cite{chuang-2023-debiasing on ViT-B/16, ViT-B/32, ViT-L/14 and ViT-H/14 backbones, respectively.}}
elements.sent-2411-164: CLIP-clip relies on the training dataset to estimate the mutual information between image embedding dimensions and the attribute labels.
elements.sent-2411-165: It then removes the most biased dimensions with the largest amount of mutual information.
elements.sent-2411-166: Biased-prompts is training-free and calculates a calibrated projection matrix based on biased text prompts to debias text embeddings.
elements.sent-2411-167: All methods were trained to remove either one of the three common types of social biases: gender, age and race.
elements.sent-2411-168: The results for gender and age debiasing are shown in Table~\ref{table:debiasing_gender_fairface and Table~\ref{table:debiasing_gender_utkface .}}
elements.sent-2411-169: Kindly refer to the supplementary material for more results.
elements.sent-2411-171: Previous debiasing methods such as CLIP-clip~\cite{wang-etal-2021-gender and Biased-prompts~\cite{chuang-2023-debiasing achieve significant bias reduction but lead to a drastic drop in V-L zero-shot performance, \ie, with a lower ABLE score.}}
elements.sent-2411-172: In contrast, our method obtains the best ABLE across most settings, suggesting that it achieves a better trade-off between debiasing and V-L alignment.
elements.sent-2411-173: Specifically, our method exhibits comparable or even better debiasing capabilities in both in-domain and out-of-domain fairness evaluations, showcasing its superior generalizability.
elements.sent-2411-174: In terms of V-L alignment, our method preserves the original CLIP's zero-shot performance with less than 1 point of drop in classification accuracy and retrieval R@5 scores.
elements.sent-2411-175: It sometimes even demonstrates equal or better V-L performance than the original CLIP model.

elements.sent-2411-176: To provide more evidence of bias removal beyond quantitative results, we use t-SNE to visualize the visual bias embedding $\psi(v_i)$ corresponding to $v_i$ in the training batch before and after module training.
elements.sent-2411-177: We observe in Fig.~\ref{fig:vis_results that after training with our bias alignment and counterfactual debiasing objectives, the image bias embeddings removed from the original embeddings carry significant bias information and can be clustered according to specific social groups of gender, age and race.}
elements.sent-2411-178: We conduct detailed ablation studies under various debiasing setups and show the results in Table~\ref{table:ablation_study_gender , Table~\ref{table:ablation_study_age and Table~\ref{table:ablation_study_race .}}}
elements.sent-2411-179: We observe that our method without the counterfactual debiasing loss leads to a great degradation in performance, especially for debiasing.
elements.sent-2411-180: This suggests that the counterfactual debiasing objective is critical in guiding the removal of image and text biases.
elements.sent-2411-181: Second, our method without the bias alignment operation also suffers a drop in debiasing together with a significant degradation of V-L alignment.
elements.sent-2411-182: This further highlights the necessity of aligning image and text biases before debiasing.
elements.sent-2411-183: Combining both losses delivers an improved balance of debiasing and V-L performance with the highest ABLE score.
elements.sent-2411-184: We also study whether our method can be applied for reducing multiple types of social biases at the same time, which is not explored in previous studies such as CLIP-clip and Biased-prompts.
elements.sent-2411-185: The debiasing is conducted by varying all attributes in the biased text prompts simultaneously during training.
elements.sent-2411-186: The results are shown in Table~\ref{table:joint_debiasing .}
elements.sent-2411-187: Our model has achieved the joint removal of all three types of social biases while maintaining the V-L performance.
elements.sent-2411-188: This work presents a comprehensive study of social bias removal in CLIP.
elements.sent-2411-189: We first revisit several existing methods and point out their over-debiasing problem.
elements.sent-2411-190: We then leverage probing tests to illuminate the innate reason for this problem.
elements.sent-2411-191: In light of our preliminary findings, we propose a debiasing method that aligns the biases in embeddings, accompanied by delicately designed counterfactual debiasing.
elements.sent-2411-192: To fairly evaluate the bias removal effect, we also advocate two evaluation protocols that involve a new holistic metric and a generalization test operation.
elements.sent-2411-193: Our experiments demonstrate that the proposed method achieves a better trade-off between CLIP model debiasing and V-L alignment.. { \bibliographystyle{ieeenat_fullname.}}
